settings.text: If you have a question, please consult our list of frequently asked questions before reaching out for assistance.

settings.question: Can you Mail In Film?
settings.answer:
You can mail us in your film. Although we recommend finding a local lab if you have one!

Only ship to:

Developing @ BFC

697 Grand St #119

Brooklyn, NY 11211

Any other address will not reach us.

Include your order number inside your package.


Fill out all options and pay for return shipping if you want your negatives returned.


Want the negatives discarded instead? Choose Pick up in store on the shipping page.

settings.question: Visit & Hours
settings.answer: Open every day 11:00–8:00. Find us at 855 Grand Street, Brooklyn, NY 11211. Get directions.

settings.question: Order Pickup
settings.answer: Pickup is available during all open hours. We’ll email you when your order is fulfilled and ready.

settings.question: Returns, Exchanges & Warranty
settings.answer: See our full returns and exchanges policy here. Unless otherwise states for individual products, Polaroid cameras include a 6-month warranty, SLR cameras include a 6 month warranty, point & shoots include a 3 month warranty ; excludes body damage, drops, and water/moisture damage.

settings.question: Camera Issues & Support
settings.answer: If your camera develops an issue within warranty, we’ll repair or exchange it after assessment. Contact SX70@brooklynfilmcamera.com.

settings.question: Shipping Worldwide
settings.answer: We ship internationally to most regions.

settings.question: Film Development & Scanning
settings.answer: In-store drop-off during open hours. You can mail us your film as well.

settings.question: Repair Coverage
settings.answer: We service most cameras through two departments: Polaroid and Everything Else (mostly 35mm & Medium Format)

settings.question: Sell Us Your Gear
settings.answer: Sell in person at our Brooklyn location. Please email photos first to griffin@brooklynfilmcamera.com.

settings.question: Turnaround Time
settings.answer: Typical turnaround is 5–12 days from receipt. Submit a repair order to see current estimates.

settings.question: I shipped you my camera! Did you get it?
settings.answer: If tracking shows delivered, it should be with us. For assurance, include your order number in your account or reply to order emails.

settings.question: New Issue After Servicing
settings.answer: All cameras are fully serviced. If something unexpected occurs afterward, contact us so we can assess and resolve it quickly. All of our services come with warranty.

settings.question: Repair Warranty Details
settings.answer: Covers additional repairs within 6 months of the repair date. It does not cover body damage, impact/drops, or water/moisture damage.

settings.question: If a Repair Isn’t Possible
settings.answer: If a repair isn’t feasible, we’ll reach out to discuss next steps with you.

settings.question: Why We Don’t Repair 600
settings.answer: Due to price point and aging plastic parts, 600-series repairs typically aren’t practical.

settings.question: Clean Your SX-70
settings.answer:
Keep the exterior clean and rollers spotless to prevent streaks, incomplete spread, or ejection issues.

Watch video

settings.question: Won’t Power On
settings.answer:
Often the film-pack battery. Try a fresh pack, clean contacts, and reseat the pack to jump-start.

Watch video

settings.question: Exposure Too Dark/Light
settings.answer:
May indicate a meter needing service. Learn your camera’s tendencies and use the lighten/darken wheel to compensate.

Watch video

settings.question: Won’t Close Fully
settings.answer:
Usually a mirror stuck mid-cycle. Carefully reset by cycling the mechanism; don’t force the panels shut.

Watch video

settings.question: Open & Close
settings.answer:
Follow the correct sequence to protect hinges and keep the linkage aligned.

Watch video

settings.question: Flash Bar Issues
settings.answer:
Clean the flash-port contacts and ensure a firm seat. Oxidation can block communication.

Watch video

settings.question: Light Leaks
settings.answer:
Often from bellows wear. Inspect in a dark room with a small flashlight inside the bellows to locate leaks.

Watch video

settings.question: 600 Film Mod
settings.answer: Lets your SX-70 shoot Polaroid 600 film (faster ISO, widely available). This is a permanent board-level change.

settings.question: Replacement Board Projects
settings.answer: Still in development.

settings.question: Warranty Coverage
settings.answer: Point & shoots typically come with 3 month warranty. SLR and Medium Format and Lenses typically 6 months. Specific items have speciality warranties. See individual items. For issues with your item please contact Repairs@brooklynfilmcamera.com.

settings.question: Repair Process
settings.answer: Order a repair quote and drop off your camera. Many repairs are completed in about a week.

settings.question: What We Repair
settings.answer: We repair most camera types. See details in our repair coverage blog post.

settings.question: SX-70 vs 600 Film
settings.answer: SX-70 film has an ISO of 160, while 600 film has an ISO of 640, meaning images will only properly expose in cameras designed for them. SX-70 film has higher contrast, richer saturation, and finer grain, but is only compatible with SX-70 folding and box cameras. 600 film is compatible with all other integral Polaroid cameras as well as the SLR 680.

settings.question: Benefits of 600 Mod
settings.answer:
Better Low-light: 600 film allows more settings without flash.
Easier to Find: Stocked widely, easier to source than SX-70.
Special Editions: Polaroid releases many special 600 films, while SX-70 is limited to core Color and B&W.

settings.question: SX-70 vs 600 Cameras
settings.answer: Most Polaroid 600 cameras have plastic lenses and fixed focal zones. The SX-70 is a higher-end tool with glass optics and precise manual focusing. The exception is the SLR 680, which combines SX-70 features with 600 film compatibility.

settings.question: Exposure Compensation
settings.answer: Adjust toward white to increase exposure, toward black for darker exposures. Useful when backlighting tricks the meter into underexposing your subject.

settings.question: Why Modern Film Looks Different
settings.answer: The original Polaroid went bankrupt in 2008. The Impossible Project (now Polaroid) revived film with different chemistry due to supply and environmental restrictions. Today’s film is constantly improving but won’t exactly match 70s–90s stock.

settings.question: Shielding Film
settings.answer: Modern film is more light-sensitive after ejection. Shield from strong light for at least 5 minutes to avoid burns, shifts, or fading.

settings.question: Instax in Polaroid?
settings.answer: No — Instax film is designed for Fujifilm Instax cameras only and cannot be used in Polaroid bodies.

settings.question: Do You Sell New Polaroid Cameras?
settings.answer: Yes, we sell all current Polaroid models.

settings.question: Yashica TLR Models
settings.answer:
Yashica A: 1/25–1/300s, f/3.5–f/22.
Yashica C: Bulb–1/300s, f/3.5–f/22.
Yashica D: 1–1/500s + bulb, lock and ergonomic upgrades.
Yashica Mat-124: Modeled after Rolleiflex, 4-element lens, built-in meter (now unreliable).
Yashica Mat-124G: Final TLR, cosmetic updates, 1–1/500s shutter, unreliable internal meter.

settings.question: Do You Sell SLRs?
settings.answer: Yes! We stock models like the Pentax K1000, Nikon F3, and more. Inventory updates often, so check back regularly.

settings.question: Film Development & Scanning
settings.answer: Yes, we offer film drop-off services during open hours. Services are in-store only (no mail-ins). Learn more.

settings.question: I Shot a Blank Roll
settings.answer:
Common causes include:
• Film not advancing properly
• Damaged/misaligned mirror or mechanisms
• Degraded light seals exposing the roll

settings.dek: Quick details about visiting, orders, repairs, and film services.
settings.font: Inter, system-ui, -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Helvetica Neue, Arial

settings.question: How much is shipping
settings.answer: We offer free Shipping on all US orders over $399.

settings.question: Where are you located and what are your hours?
settings.answer: Open every day 11:00–8:00. Find us at 855 Grand Street, Brooklyn, NY 11211.

settings.question: How does in-store order pickup work?
settings.answer: Pickup is available during all open hours. We'll email you when your order is fulfilled and ready to collect.

settings.question: Do you ship internationally?
settings.answer: Yes — we ship worldwide.

settings.question: Can I sell you my gear?
settings.answer: Absolutely. Bring your gear in person to our Brooklyn location. Please email photos first to hello@brooklynfilmcamera.com so we can give you a heads-up on interest before you make the trip.

settings.question: What is your returns and warranty policy?
settings.answer:
Polaroid cameras and SLRs include a 6-month warranty; point-and-shoots include a 3-month warranty. Warranty excludes body damage, drops, and water or moisture damage. See our full policy at brooklynfilmcamera.com/pages/warranty.
Returns on cameras and general merchandise are accepted within 14 days, free of restocking fees, with the customer covering return shipping. Items must come back in the exact same condition or a 15% restocking fee may apply. Film returns are accepted on in-store purchases only, within 72 hours, in sealed original packaging; online film orders are final sale.

settings.question: Can I mail in my film for developing?
settings.answer: Yes! Drop your film off in-store during open hours, or mail it in. To mail your film, purchase your developing service online first, then use our Service Portal to generate a pre-paid shipping label. Include your order number inside the package. Want your negatives returned? Select your preferred return shipping option at checkout. Prefer not to keep them? Choose 'Pick up in store' on the shipping page and we'll handle disposal.

settings.question: What film formats do you develop?
settings.answer: We develop 35mm, 120, and other common formats. Check our Film Processing page for the full current menu.

settings.question: What is the repair process?
settings.answer: Order a repair quote online and drop off your camera at our Grand Street workshop — or use our Service Portal to generate a pre-paid shipping label and mail it in. Our technician performs a thorough diagnostic assessment, then contacts you with a detailed quote. No work proceeds until you approve. Once approved, repairs are completed and the camera is fully tested before being returned to you.

settings.question: What is the $50 bench fee and is it refundable?
settings.answer: The $50 bench fee covers our technician's time to thoroughly assess your camera. It is non-refundable, but is applied toward your final repair cost if you proceed. If a repair isn't possible for any reason, the bench fee is still non-refundable.

settings.question: What cameras do you repair?
settings.answer: We repair most 35mm and medium format film cameras — Canon, Nikon, Pentax, Olympus, Minolta, Contax, Leica, Hasselblad, Mamiya, Bronica, Rolleiflex, Yashica, and many more. We service through two departments: Polaroid, and Everything Else. If you're unsure whether we service your model, contact us before submitting.

settings.question: How long does a repair take?
settings.answer: Standard turnaround is 2–4 weeks from receipt. Complex repairs or parts sourcing may take longer. We don't guarantee a specific timeframe as multiple factors affect timing.

settings.question: I shipped my camera — did you receive it?
settings.answer: If your tracking shows delivered, it's with us. For confirmation, include your order number inside the package, or reference it in your account. You can also reply to any of your order emails and we'll confirm.

settings.question: What does your repair warranty cover?
settings.answer: All repairs are covered for 6 months from the repair date. The warranty covers additional repairs on the original issue. It does not cover body damage, drops, or water and moisture damage.

settings.question: What if my camera develops a new issue after being serviced?
settings.answer: Contact us and we'll assess it quickly. All of our services come with warranty, and we stand behind our work.

settings.question: What if my repair isn't possible?
settings.answer: We'll reach out to you directly to discuss your options and next steps. The bench fee is non-refundable in this case.

settings.question: Why don't you repair Polaroid 600-series cameras?
settings.answer: Due to the low price point of 600-series cameras and their increasingly brittle aging plastic parts, repairs are typically not practical or cost-effective for the customer.

settings.question: What payment methods do you accept for repairs?
settings.answer: We accept all major credit cards, PayPal, and Shop Pay. The bench fee is collected when you submit your repair request. The remaining balance is due upon receiving your quote and approving the repair.

settings.question: My SX-70 won't power on — what should I try?
settings.answer: The most common cause is a depleted film-pack battery. Try a fresh pack, clean the battery contacts inside the camera, and reseat the pack firmly. This often jump-starts the camera.

settings.question: My exposures are too dark or too light — what's happening?
settings.answer: This may indicate a meter that needs service. In the meantime, learn your camera's tendencies and use the lighten/darken compensation wheel to correct. If it's consistently off by a large margin, it likely needs a CLA.

settings.question: My SX-70 won't close fully.
settings.answer: This is usually a mirror stuck mid-cycle. Carefully reset it by slowly cycling the mechanism with the camera open. Do not force the panels shut — you can damage the hinges.

settings.question: How do I properly open and close my SX-70?
settings.answer: Follow the correct sequence to protect the hinges and keep the linkage aligned. Opening and closing improperly is a common cause of mechanical issues over time.

settings.question: How do I clean my SX-70?
settings.answer: Keep the exterior clean and the rollers spotless. Dirty rollers are a leading cause of streaks, incomplete chemical spread, and ejection issues.

settings.question: My flash bar isn't working.
settings.answer: Clean the flash-port contacts on both the camera and the flash bar, and make sure the bar is firmly seated. Oxidation on the contacts is the most common culprit.

settings.question: I'm seeing light leaks in my photos.
settings.answer: Light leaks on an SX-70 are often caused by bellows wear. Inspect in a dark room using a small flashlight held inside the open bellows — you'll see pinhole leaks if they're there.

settings.question: What is the 600 film mod?
settings.answer: The 600 mod allows your SX-70 to shoot Polaroid 600 film, which has a higher ISO of 640 and is widely available. This is a permanent board-level electronic modification — it cannot be undone. Benefits include better low-light performance, easier film sourcing, and access to Polaroid's many special edition 600 releases.

settings.question: What is the difference between SX-70 and 600 film?
settings.answer: SX-70 film has an ISO of 160; 600 film has an ISO of 640. SX-70 film delivers higher contrast, richer saturation, and finer grain, but only works in SX-70 folding and box cameras. 600 film is compatible with all other integral Polaroid cameras, plus the SLR 680.

settings.question: What is the difference between SX-70 and 600 cameras?
settings.answer: Most 600 cameras feature plastic lenses and fixed focal zones. The SX-70 is a precision instrument with glass optics and manual focus control. The exception is the SLR 680, which combines SX-70 build quality with 600 film compatibility.

settings.question: How does exposure compensation work on a Polaroid?
settings.answer: Adjust the lighten/darken wheel toward white to increase exposure, toward black for less. This is especially useful in backlit situations where the meter may underexpose your subject.

settings.question: Why does modern Polaroid film look different from vintage shots?
settings.answer: The original Polaroid Corporation went bankrupt in 2008. The Impossible Project (now rebranded as Polaroid) revived instant film using different chemistry due to supply constraints and environmental regulations. Today's film is constantly improving, but will not be an exact match for 1970s–90s stock.

settings.question: Why do I need to shield my film after it ejects?
settings.answer: Modern instant film is more sensitive to light in the seconds immediately after ejection. Shield from strong light for at least 5 minutes to prevent burns, color shifts, or fading.

settings.question: Can I use Instax film in a Polaroid camera?
settings.answer: No. Instax film is designed exclusively for Fujifilm Instax cameras and cannot be used in any Polaroid body.

settings.question: Do you sell new Polaroid cameras?
settings.answer: Yes — we stock all current Polaroid models. Check our Polaroid collection for what's available.

settings.question: Do you sell SLRs?
settings.answer: Yes! We stock models like the Pentax K1000, Nikon F3, Canon AE-1 Program, and more. Our inventory updates frequently — check back regularly or sign up for restock alerts.

settings.question: What are the differences between Yashica TLR models?
settings.answer: Yashica A: 1/25–1/300s, f/3.5–f/22. Yashica C: Bulb–1/300s, f/3.5–f/22. Yashica D: 1–1/500s with bulb mode, plus lock and ergonomic upgrades. Yashica Mat-124: Modeled after the Rolleiflex, 4-element lens, built-in meter (now unreliable). Yashica Mat-124G: Final TLR iteration — cosmetic updates, 1–1/500s shutter, unreliable internal meter.

settings.subtitle: Quick answers on repairs, film developing, cameras, and visiting us.
